examples.title: Algorithm: Data Structure Classification and Operations
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the classification of primitive and non-primitive data structures with basic operations
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DataStructureClassification
INPUT: None (demonstrates classification)
OUTPUT: Classification overview and operation examples

//================== PRIMITIVE DATA STRUCTURES ==================
BEGIN
 // Primitive types are predefined by the language
 DECLARE integer age // Stores whole numbers
 DECLARE float price // Stores decimal numbers
 DECLARE character grade // Stores single character
 DECLARE boolean flag // Stores true/false
 DECLARE pointer addr // Stores memory address
END

//================== NON-PRIMITIVE: LINEAR ==================
ALGORITHM ArrayOperations
INPUT: array of integers
OUTPUT: Demonstrates array operations

BEGIN
 DECLARE ARRAY numbers[5] = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
 
 // Access by index - O(1)
 PRINT numbers[0] // First element
 PRINT numbers[4] // Last element
 
 // Traverse - O(n)
 FOR i FROM 0 TO 4 DO
 PRINT numbers[i]
 END FOR
END

ALGORITHM LinkedListOperations
INPUT: None
OUTPUT: Demonstrates linked list node structure

BEGIN
 // Node structure
 DECLARE STRUCT Node
 DATA integer
 NEXT pointer to Node
 END STRUCT
 
 // Create nodes
 DECLARE Node node1, node2, node3
 node1.data = 10
 node2.data = 20
 node3.data = 30
 
 // Link nodes
 node1.next = &node2
 node2.next = &node3
 node3.next = NULL
 
 // Traverse - O(n)
 DECLARE temp = &node1
 WHILE temp != NULL DO
 PRINT temp.data
 temp = temp.next
 END WHILE
END

ALGORITHM StackOperations
INPUT: elements to push
OUTPUT: Demonstrates LIFO operations

BEGIN
 DECLARE STACK s
 
 // Push - O(1)
 PUSH(s, 10)
 PUSH(s, 20)
 PUSH(s, 30)
 
 // Pop - O(1)
 PRINT POP(s) // Outputs: 30
 PRINT POP(s) // Outputs: 20
 
 // Peek
 PRINT TOP(s) // Outputs: 10
END

ALGORITHM QueueOperations
INPUT: elements to enqueue
OUTPUT: Demonstrates FIFO operations

BEGIN
 DECLARE QUEUE q
 
 // Enqueue - O(1)
 ENQUEUE(q, 10)
 ENQUEUE(q, 20)
 ENQUEUE(q, 30)
 
 // Dequeue - O(1)
 PRINT DEQUEUE(q) // Outputs: 10
 PRINT DEQUEUE(q) // Outputs: 20
 
 // Front
 PRINT FRONT(q) // Outputs: 30
END

//================== NON-PRIMITIVE: NON-LINEAR ==================
ALGORITHM TreeOperations
INPUT: None
OUTPUT: Demonstrates binary tree node

BEGIN
 DECLARE STRUCT TreeNode
 DATA integer
 LEFT pointer to TreeNode
 RIGHT pointer to TreeNode
 END STRUCT
 
 // Create tree: 10
 // / \
 // 5 15
 DECLARE TreeNode root, left, right
 root.data = 10
 left.data = 5
 right.data = 15
 root.left = &left
 root.right = &right
END

ALGORITHM GraphOperations
INPUT: None
OUTPUT: Demonstrates graph representation

BEGIN
 // Adjacency List Representation
 DECLARE GRAPH g
 
 // Add vertices
 ADD_VERTEX(g, 'A')
 ADD_VERTEX(g, 'B')
 ADD_VERTEX(g, 'C')
 
 // Add edges (undirected)
 ADD_EDGE(g, 'A', 'B')
 ADD_EDGE(g, 'B', 'C')
 ADD_EDGE(g, 'A', 'C')
 
 // Graph: A
 // / \
 // B---C
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the classification of data structures. Primitive types (integer, float, character, boolean, pointer) are basic building blocks. Non-primitive structures include linear (arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues) and non-linear (trees, graphs) types. Each algorithm shows the fundamental operations and time complexities.

examples.title: Python: Implementation of Primitive and Non-Primitive Data Structures
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating primitive data types and various non-primitive data structures
examples.code:
"""
Python Implementation: Primitive and Non-Primitive Data Structures
2022 Scheme - Data Structures Topic
"""

# ================== PRIMITIVE DATA STRUCTURES ==================
# Primitive types are predefined by Python

# Integer - whole numbers
age = 25
temperature = -5
print(f"Integer examples: age={age}, temperature={temperature}")

# Float - decimal numbers
price = 99.99
pi = 3.14159265
print(f"Float examples: price={price}, pi={pi}")

# Character (in Python, strings are used)
grade = 'A'
symbol = '@'
print(f"Character examples: grade={grade}, symbol={symbol}")

# Boolean - True/False
isPassed = True
isRaining = False
print(f"Boolean examples: isPassed={isPassed}, isRaining={isRaining}")

# Pointer equivalent in Python (references)
# In Python, variables are references to objects
original = [1, 2, 3] # List object
copy_ref = original # Both point to same object
print(f"Pointer equivalent: original={original}, copy_ref={copy_ref}")
print(f"Same object? {original is copy_ref}")


# ================== NON-PRIMITIVE: LINEAR DATA STRUCTURES ==================

# ---------- Arrays (Python lists) ----------
class ArrayDemo:
 def __init__(self):
 # Fixed-size array concept using list
 self.marks = [85, 90, 78, 92, 88]
 
 def access(self, index):
 """Access by index - O(1)"""
 return self.marks[index]
 
 def traverse(self):
 """Traverse all elements - O(n)"""
 for i, mark in enumerate(self.marks):
 print(f"Index {i}: {mark}")
 
 def search(self, target):
 """Linear search - O(n)"""
 for i, mark in enumerate(self.marks):
 if mark == target:
 return i
 return -1


# ---------- Linked List ----------
class Node:
 """Node for linked list"""
 def __init__(self, data):
 self.data = data
 self.next = None

class LinkedList:
 """Singly Linked List implementation"""
 def __init__(self):
 self.head = None
 
 def append(self, data):
 """Add to end - O(n)"""
 new_node = Node(data)
 if not self.head:
 self.head = new_node
 return
 current = self.head
 while current.next:
 current = current.next
 current.next = new_node
 
 def prepend(self, data):
 """Add to beginning - O(1)"""
 new_node = Node(data)
 new_node.next = self.head
 self.head = new_node
 
 def delete(self, data):
 """Delete node - O(n)"""
 if not self.head:
 return
 if self.head.data == data:
 self.head = self.head.next
 return
 current = self.head
 while current.next:
 if current.next.data == data:
 current.next = current.next.next
 return
 current = current.next
 
 def display(self):
 """Traverse and display - O(n)"""
 elements = []
 current = self.head
 while current:
 elements.append(str(current.data))
 current = current.next
 print(" -> ".join(elements) if elements else "Empty")
 
 def search(self, target):
 """Search - O(n)"""
 current = self.head
 position = 0
 while current:
 if current.data == target:
 return position
 current = current.next
 position += 1
 return -1


# ---------- Stack (LIFO) ----------
class Stack:
 """Stack implementation using list"""
 def __init__(self):
 self.items = []
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """Check if stack is empty - O(1)"""
 return len(self.items) == 0
 
 def push(self, item):
 """Add to top - O(1)"""
 self.items.append(item)
 
 def pop(self):
 """Remove from top - O(1)"""
 if not self.is_empty():
 return self.items.pop()
 return None
 
 def peek(self):
 """View top element - O(1)"""
 return self.items[-1] if self.items else None
 
 def size(self):
 """Return stack size - O(1)"""
 return len(self.items)


# ---------- Queue (FIFO) ----------
class Queue:
 """Queue implementation using list"""
 def __init__(self):
 self.items = []
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """Check if queue is empty - O(1)"""
 return len(self.items) == 0
 
 def enqueue(self, item):
 """Add to rear - O(1)"""
 self.items.append(item)
 
 def dequeue(self):
 """Remove from front - O(n) - O(1) with collections.deque"""
 if not self.is_empty():
 return self.items.pop(0)
 return None
 
 def front(self):
 """View front element - O(1)"""
 return self.items[0] if self.items else None
 
 def size(self):
 """Return queue size - O(1)"""
 return len(self.items)


# ================== NON-PRIMITIVE: NON-LINEAR ==================

# ---------- Binary Tree ----------
class TreeNode:
 """Tree node for binary tree"""
 def __init__(self, data):
 self.data = data
 self.left = None
 self.right = None

class BinaryTree:
 """Binary Tree implementation"""
 def __init__(self, root_data):
 self.root = TreeNode(root_data)
 
 def insert_left(self, parent, data):
 """Insert left child - O(1)"""
 parent.left = TreeNode(data)
 
 def insert_right(self, parent, data):
 """Insert right child - O(1)"""
 parent.right = TreeNode(data)
 
 def inorder(self, node):
 """Inorder traversal - O(n)"""
 if node:
 self.inorder(node.left)
 print(node.data, end=' ')
 self.inorder(node.right)
 
 def preorder(self, node):
 """Preorder traversal - O(n)"""
 if node:
 print(node.data, end=' ')
 self.preorder(node.left)
 self.preorder(node.right)
 
 def postorder(self, node):
 """Postorder traversal - O(n)"""
 if node:
 self.postorder(node.left)
 self.postorder(node.right)
 print(node.data, end=' ')


# ---------- Graph ----------
class Graph:
 """Graph implementation using adjacency list"""
 def __init__(self):
 self.adjacency_list = {}
 
 def add_vertex(self, vertex):
 """Add a vertex - O(1)"""
 if vertex not in self.adjacency_list:
 self.adjacency_list[vertex] = []
 
 def add_edge(self, v1, v2, directed=False):
 """Add an edge - O(1)"""
 self.add_vertex(v1)
 self.add_vertex(v2)
 self.adjacency_list[v1].append(v2)
 if not directed:
 self.adjacency_list[v2].append(v1)
 
 def display(self):
 """Display graph - O(V + E)"""
 for vertex, edges in self.adjacency_list.items():
 print(f"{vertex} -> {edges}")


# ================== DEMONSTRATION ==================
def main():
 print("=" * 60)
 print("PRIMITIVE DATA TYPES DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 60)
 # Already demonstrated above
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("NON-PRIMITIVE: LINKED LIST DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 60)
 ll = LinkedList()
 ll.append(10)
 ll.append(20)
 ll.append(30)
 print("Linked List:", end=" ")
 ll.display()
 ll.prepend(5)
 print("After prepending 5:", end=" ")
 ll.display()
 print(f"Search 20 at position: {ll.search(20)}")
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("NON-PRIMITIVE: STACK DEMONSTRATION (LIFO)")
 print("=" * 60)
 stack = Stack()
 stack.push(10)
 stack.push(20)
 stack.push(30)
 print(f"Stack size: {stack.size()}")
 print(f"Pop: {stack.pop()}") # 30
 print(f"Pop: {stack.pop()}") # 20
 print(f"Peek: {stack.peek()}") # 10
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("NON-PRIMITIVE: QUEUE DEMONSTRATION (FIFO)")
 print("=" * 60)
 queue = Queue()
 queue.enqueue(10)
 queue.enqueue(20)
 queue.enqueue(30)
 print(f"Queue size: {queue.size()}")
 print(f"Dequeue: {queue.dequeue()}") # 10
 print(f"Dequeue: {queue.dequeue()}") # 20
 print(f"Front: {queue.front()}") # 30
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("NON-PRIMITIVE: BINARY TREE DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 60)
 # Create tree: 10
 # / \
 # 5 15
 bt = BinaryTree(10)
 bt.insert_left(bt.root, 5)
 bt.insert_right(bt.root, 15)
 print("Inorder traversal:", end=" ")
 bt.inorder(bt.root) # 5 10 15
 print("\nPreorder traversal:", end=" ")
 bt.preorder(bt.root) # 10 5 15
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("NON-PRIMITIVE: GRAPH DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 60)
 # Create graph: A
 # / \
 # B C
 graph = Graph()
 graph.add_edge('A', 'B')
 graph.add_edge('A', 'C')
 graph.add_edge('B', 'C')
 graph.display()


if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates both primitive and non-primitive data structures. Primitive types (int, float, str, bool) are built-in. Non-primitive structures include: Arrays (lists), Linked Lists (custom Node class), Stacks (LIFO), Queues (FIFO), Binary Trees, and Graphs (adjacency list). Each structure includes common operations with their time complexities.
examples.timeComplexity: Varies by operation: O(1) for stack push/pop, O(n) for linked list traversal
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing n elements in all structures

examples.title: C: Implementation of Primitive and Non-Primitive Data Structures
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating primitive data types and various non-primitive data structures with memory management
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates both primitive and non-primitive data structures. Primitive types (int, float, double, char, bool, pointers) are built-in types. Non-primitive structures include: Arrays (fixed-size), Linked List (dynamic with malloc), Stack (LIFO with array), Queue (FIFO with circular array), Binary Tree (recursive structure), and Graph (adjacency matrix). Each includes memory management with malloc/free and demonstrates key operations with their time complexities.
examples.timeComplexity: Varies: O(1) for stack/queue push-pop, O(n) for traversal operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing n elements; O(n^2) for graph adjacency matrix
